topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, cream-colored paper felt alien in my trembling hands. It was my essay, the one I poured weeks of late-night introspection into—a rambling, earnest piece about navigating loss. But the font was bolder, the paragraphs tighter, and beneath the familiar prose, a stark, almost clinical citation marked the bottom corner: "Example Submission: Successful Narrative Arc." A cold knot tightened in my chest, squeezing the air from my lungs. I reread the opening sentence, the words tasting like ash. It wasn't mine. It was someone else's polished reflection, a trophy taken from my own vulnerability. I slowly folded the paper, the sound of the crease sharp in the quiet dorm room. My fingers felt clumsy, unresponsive, as if they’d forgotten how to grip anything properly. I stared at the blank wall opposite, the silence amplifying the dull throb behind my eyes.

[story 2]

I found it tucked inside a stack of discarded drafts, a ghost of my own ambition. The essay was flawless, a masterpiece of eloquent self-portrayal. It spoke of resilience and profound insight, the very qualities I desperately hoped to convey. Then, I saw the small, almost invisible watermark—a university logo I didn't recognize, and a note scrawled in fine, elegant script: "Illustrative Piece." A wave of nausea washed over me. My breath hitched, shallow and fast. I dropped the paper onto the worn wooden desk. I leaned back in my chair, my spine rigid, trying to steady the sudden, dizzying imbalance. Every muscle in my jaw clenched. It felt like a physical blow, a sudden, crushing weight settling on my sternum, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I just sat there, watching the dust motes dance in the afternoon sun, unable to move, the world seeming muted and distant.

[story 3]

It was an accidental discovery, a misplaced document unearthed from the depths of an old binder. My eyes scanned the page, and the familiar rhythm of my own thoughts was suddenly overlaid by someone else’s perfectly modulated voice. The narrative flowed too smoothly, too confidently. I traced a line of text with my fingertip, the texture of the paper rough against my skin, a tangible reminder of the deception. A strange hollowness expanded in my core. I stood up abruptly, knocking over a water glass; the resulting splash seemed disproportionately loud in the small space. My hands shook so violently I had to press them against my thighs to keep them still. I walked to the window, the cityscape blurring into an indistinct smear of color. The air felt too thin, and a profound sense of displacement settled over me, leaving a residue of aching emptiness where excitement used to reside.
